Wise Traditions · Weston A. Price Foundation

The Bulb Your Grandmother Bought

How a century-old, health-preserving technology was withdrawn from American homes — and what the Constitution says about getting it back.

By Roleigh Martin

The aisle is forty feet long, and every bulb on it is an LED.

Walk it the way an older American walks it, looking for the warm filament glow the house has used since before the war. It is not there. The soft, golden, full-spectrum light that a grandmother screwed into a kitchen fixture, that a father read newspapers under, that warmed a child's bedroom to sleep — that light is gone from the residential shelf. In its place stands a wall of white and blue-white LEDs, engineered for one number on the box: lumens per watt.

Its foundational patents expired in the 1890s. From 1879 to 2022 it sat on every hardware-store shelf in the country. American households bought it for less than a dollar. Then, between 2007 and 2024, a sequence of federal rules made it effectively illegal to manufacture for ordinary home use.

The warm light we lost was better for the body than the blue-shifted light that replaced it. The bulb never had to be taken away, the Constitution did not permit it to be taken the way it was, and the same Constitution — read as the Supreme Court has already read it — can bring it back. The health case is the reason to care. The constitutional case is the reason it can be fixed.

THE LIGHT WE WERE BUILT FOR

Begin with the body.

Human beings evolved under sun and fire, both rich in long-wavelength red and near-infrared light; the incandescent bulb, heating a filament, carries that same warm, continuous spectrum, while the blue-shifted LED that replaced it spikes toward blue and runs thin in the red and infrared the body has always received. Readers here know what follows. The red and near-infrared wavelengths are absorbed by the mitochondria and raise cellular energy — Dr. Glen Jeffery's work at University College London found that brief exposure to deep-red light near 670 nanometers measurably improved retinal function in aging eyes,1, 2 and the wider field of photobiomodulation, gathered in Michael Hamblin's 2017 review,3 documents anti-inflammatory and energy effects across many tissues, a body of work Dr. Andrew Huberman has brought to a wide audience.4 At night the account reverses: the circadian system reads blue as daylight, so a home lit blue after dark suppresses melatonin and tells the internal clock that midnight is noon. The warm bulb, low in blue, let the evening stay evening.

For most households the loss is one of comfort and, over years, of health worth protecting. For some Americans it is not a preference at all. It is medical. The Epilepsy Foundation's 2022 review documents that roughly three percent of people with epilepsy have seizures triggered by flickering light, the worst of it in a low-frequency band some LEDs produce5 — on the order of a hundred thousand Americans for whom a steady filament bulb is safety, not nostalgia. Add the seasonal-mood, psoriasis, and blue-sensitive patients whose care depended on controlled-spectrum or incandescent sources.6 The federal analysis weighed none of them: its projected savings were computed for the household that can switch, and are simply unavailable to the household a physician has told not to.

WHAT IS ACTUALLY ON THE SHELF TODAY

The rule's reach is narrower than the word "ban" suggests. It is a manufacturing-and-sales restriction, not a possession ban: the bulbs already in a socket are lawful to keep using, but making, importing, or selling the ordinary bulb is forbidden. Federal law now prohibits the sale of a general-service lamp producing fewer than 45 lumens per watt — and a traditional incandescent produces only about 12 to 15, missing the mark by design. Despite widespread reports that a January 2025 executive order lifted the rule, it remains in force.

There is no medical exception. A patient whose neurologist has told him to avoid LED flicker cannot obtain a warm incandescent by prescription; no such pathway exists. What remains legal is a shrinking set of true specialty bulbs — oven and appliance lamps, bug lights, certain infrared and industrial shapes — none of them the familiar warm household bulb. The "rough service" and "shatter-resistant" workarounds people still cite were exempt before 2022 and are not now. And the rule tightens again in 2028, when a 120-lumen-per-watt floor takes effect that would sweep out most compact fluorescents and even many of today's LEDs. The direction is one way, toward fewer choices, and the household with a medical reason to want the old light has no lawful way to buy it.

IT NEVER HAD TO HAPPEN

Every injury named above was avoidable, and the avoidance was cheap. A single grandfather clause — a line of statute exempting a century-old, public-domain bulb from a standard written for new technology — would have spared the epileptic patient, the circadian patient, the psoriasis patient, and every household that simply wanted a warm evening. Governments write such exemptions all the time. The older building is not forced to meet the new code. The historic district keeps its wiring. The question worth sitting with is why the incandescent bulb got no such grace — and the answer is not that the science demanded it or that safety required it. The answer is constitutional

THE PROMISE IN THE CONSTITUTION

The Framers wrote the rule into the founding document. Article I, Section 8, Clause 8 gives Congress power to grant inventors exclusive rights — but only "for limited Times." Those two words are the whole bargain. Society grants an inventor a temporary monopoly as the price of drawing the invention out into the open; when the time runs out, the monopoly ends and the invention belongs to everyone. The reward is real but bounded. The public domain that follows is permanent.

This is not a modern gloss. It is how the Supreme Court has read the Clause for well over a century, in a line of decisions that do not wobble. In Singer Manufacturing (1896) the Court held that when a patent expires, the right to make the thing it covered "becomes public property" — and specifically the right to make the machine in the form in which it was built during the patent. In Scott Paper (1945) the Court held that the patent laws "dedicate to public use" the invention of an expired patent, and that no one may "withhold from the public the use of the invention." In Sears (1964) it held the expired-patent article "may be made and sold by whoever chooses to do so." And in Kimble v. Marvel (2015) — the most recent statement — the Court held that on expiration "the right to make or use the article, free from all restriction, passes to the public."

The Clause binds the government, too, not just private parties. In McCormick Harvesting Machine Co. v. Aultman (1898) the Court held that once a patent has issued, the very agency that granted it has no power to revoke, cancel, or annul it — only a court can. That is the holding; the next step is this argument's own. If an agency cannot cancel the inventor's side of the bargain, it is hard to see how an agency may cancel the public's side — the guaranteed reversion at expiration — which is written into the same constitutional grant. The Court has not ruled on that application; the parallel is the argument's, not the Court's. Two later cases confirm the patent is genuine property with a fixed boundary — Graham v. John Deere (1966), reading the Clause as a real limit on Congress, and Florida Prepaid (1999), confirming a patent is a species of property the law protects. The expiration is not a courtesy. It is a constitutional term, and no agency was given authority to write it out.

That phrase, "free from all restriction," means free of the expired patent's monopoly, not immune from every ordinary regulation. The public domain is not a lawless zone. But it is a real inheritance, and it means something specific — the right to make the actual thing, in the form in which it was built, not merely the right to read the old patent and admire it.

Look closer at the verb the Framers chose. The Clause does not say Congress may permit an exclusive right, or tolerate one; it says Congress may secure it — and in 1790 the First Congress read “secure” as an act of national force, directing that patents issue “in the name of the United States,” backed by a federal lawsuit in the federal courts. To secure a right is to place the government’s power behind it. But the Clause commits that power for one thing only: exclusivity for limited Times. Now see what the lighting rule does with that same power. It does not let the incumbent rebuild the old bulb either — the efficiency floor bars everyone. Yet that universal bar is precisely what hands the incumbent the whole practical benefit its expired patents once gave it: freedom from a cheaper, revived incandescent. The government is not standing aside while a monopoly lingers. It is actively securing that freedom, with the same federal force the Clause authorized only until the patents expired — and then never letting the securing stop. That the incumbent cannot make the bulb either is not the rule’s innocence; it is the mechanism of the harm. The reading of “secure” as a bounded grant of force is this argument’s own, drawn from the text and the First Congress’s practice; the Court has not passed on it.

WHAT THE EXPIRED-PATENT COMMONS IS

When a patent expires, the invention it protected falls into what is best called the expired-patent commons — the vast, growing library of technologies that once were owned and now belong to all of us. This is a narrower and more precise idea than the general public domain, which also holds ancient tools like the lever and the wheel that were never anyone's property to begin with. The expired-patent commons is specifically the set of once-patented inventions whose limited term has run: Otto's four-stroke engine, the telephone, aspirin, the transistor, the catalytic converter whose core patents lapsed in the 1990s. Each began as an inventor's exclusive, legally protected property and, when its term ended, became everyone's. This is the mechanism by which a free society compounds its inheritance. Today's inventor builds on ten thousand expired patents she never had to license, because the men and women who held them received their limited term and then, by the Constitution's design, handed the invention to posterity.

The incandescent bulb entered that commons more than a hundred years ago. Edison's foundational filament patent crossed the line by the mid-1890s; the tungsten-filament patents that defined the modern bulb crossed it in the early twentieth century. By 1925 the ordinary incandescent was, as a matter of constitutional doctrine, common property. It stayed there, freely manufactured and freely bought, for ninety-seven more years. The commons was not theoretical in residential lighting. It was an industry, and it worked exactly as the Founders intended, for one hundred and forty-three years.

WHY THE BULB IS THE CLEAREST TAKING

The constitutional case against the wall travels on two legs. First, the Patent Clause: by writing standards only the patent-holding incumbent can meet, the regulatory wall extends that incumbent's practical monopoly past the expiration "limited Times" commands, and so rewrites the Constitution's bargain. That argument does the main work for tractors, trucks, and furnaces alike. Second, the Takings Clause — the argument a litigant can win even if a court doubts the first.

For most durable goods the Patent Clause leads because the alternative is harder to establish: the regulatory ratchet blocked remanufacturing before any public right could be exercised, so a government lawyer can argue no property ever vested. The light bulb is the exception, and it is the exception that makes it the clearest taking of them all. Here the public right was not blocked before it could form. It was exercised in open commerce for 143 years and only then suppressed. That single fact — a vested right, demonstrably used at national scale, withdrawn — is what turns the bulb into the strongest takings case in the entire set, which is why this article leads with the Takings road even though the Patent Clause remains the argument's primary weapon everywhere else.

Now set the lighting rules against that backdrop, and the constitutional problem comes into focus.

The Fifth Amendment closes with a short command: "nor shall private property be taken for public use, without just compensation." The wall's defender will pounce on the first two words. Private property, he will say, is the opposite of a public commons; a right belonging to everyone is not private property, so the Clause has nothing to say here. The objection sounds decisive and is not, because it misreads what "property" means in this clause. Property here is not a physical thing but a bundle of legal rights — to make, use, sell, and exclude — and the Clause has long protected intangible forms of it, including patents, copyrights, and trade secrets. "Private" marks that bundle as belonging to those who hold it rather than to the state; it does not require that only one person hold it. The Supreme Court has applied the phrase to a patent directly: in James v. Campbell (1882) it held that the government's unauthorized use of a patented invention is a taking of private property for which the inventor must be paid. A patent is intangible and licensable to many — the features the wall's defender calls disqualifying — and the Court called it private property all the same.

So a takings argument must answer two questions in turn: what property was taken, and from whom. Neither answer is the inventor's patent — the rule does not touch a live patent, which expires on schedule exactly as the Constitution promises. What the rule destroys is the other half of the bargain: the right, arising at expiration, to make and use the invention.

That this right is property is not a stretch. In Singer Manufacturing (1896) the Court held that on expiration "the right to make the thing formerly covered by the patent becomes public property," and it said as much in Scott Paper and Bonito Boats. Those were patent cases, not takings cases — that the Fifth Amendment protects this right is the argument's own step, not their holding — but the noun is the Court's, and the right carries the classic bundle the word property names: to make, use, and sell a defined thing. That the right has no physical form is no obstacle: in Ruckelshaus v. Monsanto (1984) the Court held that an intangible right of defined economic value is property the Takings Clause protects, and that an agency does not escape the Clause because what it appropriated cannot be held in the hand.

Then comes the sharper form of the objection. The Fifth Amendment protects a person whose property is taken, and "the public" is not a person; a right held by everyone can look like a right held by no one, with no plaintiff for a court to compensate. A related objection says the right is a mere hope, since nothing exists until an American actually builds the expired design. Both fail on the same ground already laid: property is the right to do a thing, not the doing of it, so the inherited right does not evaporate for want of exercise. And a right does not stop being property because a recent regulation refuses to honor it — in Tyler v. Hennepin County (2023) the Court held that a government may not sidestep the Takings Clause by disavowing a traditional property interest it wishes to appropriate. The wall is exactly such a disavowal, and Tyler says the disavowal does not unmake the right.

The right of access belongs to the public, but it belongs to each member of the public individually, and any one member with a concrete enough stake has standing to sue as a proper takings plaintiff. The plaintiff is never "the public" in the abstract. It is a particular, injured party: a county sheriff whose department is barred from buying the remanufactured patent-expired trucks that competition would produce; a farmer denied the remanufactured tractor; a manufacturer with a factory, a workforce, and no lawful expired design to build. The Constitution does not protect this property less because it is widely held. It protects each holder.

There is even a corner of the Court's own law where the property taken was held by no individual at all, and was compensable anyway. Tribal land owned in common — titled to a people as a body, not to any member in fee — is property the Fifth Amendment protects when the federal government takes it: the Court so held for the Black Hills in United States v. Sioux Nation of Indians (1980), and Justice Cardozo had already fixed the principle in Shoshone Tribe v. United States (1937) with four words that fit the wall exactly — "Spoliation is not management." Those cases concerned Indian lands under treaty, not the patent commons, and the argument does not pretend they decided this question. It notes only what they settle: property need not be privately or individually titled to fall within the Just Compensation Clause. Extending that settled rule to the public's common right in the Expired Patent Commons is a short and logical step — one more place where the wall asks the Court to honor a principle it has already announced.

Each link in this chain rests on holdings the Supreme Court has actually issued — that the expired-patent right is property, that intangible property is protected, that a government cannot disavow a right to avoid paying for it, that a total deprivation is a taking per se. But no court has yet been asked to assemble those links around this precise fact: a public right of access, taken by regulation, compensable to the individual holder. The argument is legally complete and, in the author's view, sound. It has simply never been presented in federal court — the definition of a question of first impression. The Takings road set out here is the one this bulb makes clearest, while the Patent Clause prong is the lead argument across the wider family of patent-expired goods, reaching the same destination by a different route. The case does not stand or fall on either prong alone.

What, then, is the remedy? The Takings Clause does not forbid the government to take property; it requires payment. A ruling that the rule is a taking does not, by itself, order it repealed — it establishes that the public is owed. Across the durable-goods economy the wall built on this pattern has moved an estimated \$22 trillion from households to incumbent manufacturers, on the order of \$3,385 from the median household every year it stands.10 No Treasury can carry a standing yearly obligation of that size, which leaves the government two doors: keep the wall and pay just compensation at the scale of the taking, year after year, or lift the wall from patent-expired designs and owe nothing going forward, because once the public has its access there is no taking left to compensate. The Takings prong does not order the wall down. It makes the wall too expensive to keep standing.

The scale of what was withdrawn sharpens the point. The Department of Energy's own market survey found roughly 819 million incandescent lamps still installed in American sockets at the moment the rule moved to end their manufacture.7 The government did not block a right before it could form; it reached into a functioning, century-old commons and pulled a technology out of it, on behalf of the patent-holding companies that profit from its absence. That total erasure matters doctrinally: in Lucas v. South Carolina Coastal Council (1992) the Court held that a regulation destroying all economically viable use of a property right is a taking per se, found without the case-by-case balancing the Court applies to lesser burdens; and a rule unwilling to be called a total deprivation still faces the balancing test of Penn Central (1978), which weighs both the severity of the interference and the character of a rule enacted to benefit the patent holders. In Bonito Boats (1989) the Court reaffirmed that federal patent law creates a positive federal right to copy and use an expired design; the lighting rules do not merely burden that right, they abolish the market for its exercise.

There is a plainer way to see the inequality. The bulb your grandmother installed in 1975 is lawful forever, by the accident of when it was made; building the same bulb today is forbidden. Same design — the only variable is the date on the calendar. The company that held the patents got its exclusive years; the public was promised the years after. This is not the ordinary "old building stands, new building meets code" trade-off: the new builder is not asked to build something better but forbidden to build the specific thing the Constitution promised him — to the benefit of the very patent holders who asked for the rule. The word for that is not regulation. It is a taking.

THE LOCK THAT PROVES THE POINT

If any doubt remains that this is a taking rather than an adjustable regulation, the statute removes it. The federal appliance law contains an anti-backsliding provision: once an efficiency standard is set, the Department of Energy may not loosen it.8 The ratchet turns one way only. This was tested at the top of the executive branch — in January 2025 a presidential executive order referenced consumer choice in light bulbs, and within days the manufacturers' own association and the lighting trade press confirmed that the order could not override the statutory lock. The banned bulb stayed banned.9

A standard that a President of the United States cannot relax is not an ordinary standard. The Supreme Court has said, in Arkansas Game & Fish (2012), that the duration and permanence of a government imposition bear on whether it is a taking; a burden engineered to be permanent and irreversible carries the mark of a taking, not of ordinary regulation. Congress did not merely regulate the bulb. It built a mechanism to ensure the public-domain right could never be restored by any President, any future Congress, or any election. That designed permanence is not a defense of the rule. It is the strongest evidence against it. The wall is permanent against every actor below the constitutional level. The Supreme Court is not below that level.

THE DOOR THAT REOPENED

For forty years, a challenge like this would have died before a court reached its merits. Under the doctrine of Chevron deference, judges were required to defer to an agency's reading of an ambiguous statute, and the Department of Energy's reading would have won by default. In 2024 the Supreme Court ended that regime. Loper Bright restored to courts the duty to say what the law is rather than rubber-stamp the agency. Paired with the major-questions doctrine — under which an agency needs clear congressional authorization to decide questions of vast economic and political significance — a court is now positioned to ask the question that was previously unaskable: did Congress ever authorize the Department of Energy to write efficiency standards that function as a permanent extension of patent monopolies? It did not.

The case has simply never been filed. It is a question of first impression — not lost, but unasked.

WHEN ELEVEN MACHINES READ THE SAME CLAUSE

There is one more piece of the record worth putting before a skeptical reader, because it speaks to whether the argument is sound or merely passionate.

The core of the constitutional argument was put, unchanged, to eleven independent artificial-intelligence models — the current frontier systems, built by competing companies on different training data, including OpenCase, an engine built for legal research on a database of case law. Each was given the same materials and the same question. Each worked through the Supreme Court holdings independently. All eleven reached the same conclusion: that the public's constitutional right is not satisfied by the current regime, and that the argument raises a serious constitutional question warranting judicial review. The full exchange — every prompt, every unedited reply — is public.

That convergence decides nothing — only a court can. But it is part of the record, and it is striking that systems with no stake in the outcome, reasoning from the case law, arrive at the plaintiff's conclusion. What the exercise shows is that the argument survives adversarial examination by eleven cold readers — that a person does not have to take the author's word, or share the author's passion, to see that the question is real.

WHAT RESTORATION LOOKS LIKE

Suppose the case is brought and won. What returns?

The bulb returns — not as a museum piece, but as a manufactured product. American makers, certified to a defined engineering standard, build ordinary incandescent bulbs again at roughly the price that served the country for a century. The person whose neurologist forbids flicker buys the bulb his neurologist recommends. The household that wants a low-blue evening has one. The household that prefers an LED keeps buying LEDs, exactly as before — nothing in the remedy forces any bulb on anyone. It restores a choice that was taken away.

There is an honest complication. Since 2022 the machinery for making residential incandescents has been largely dismantled, and a bare repeal would not restart it overnight. This is one reason the durable remedy is constitutional rather than merely legislative: a ruling that establishes the right to build, under a defined certification framework, gives American manufacturers the settled ground they need to invest. The commons becomes a buildable thing again rather than a paper promise.

And the stakes reach past lighting. The same pattern — a standard written so that only patented technology can meet it, closing the market to an expired-patent alternative — recurs across durable goods, from vehicles to home heating. Independent estimates put the cumulative cost of this larger pattern to American households at some \$22 trillion since 1970, on the order of \$3,385 per household every year.10 The light bulb is one room in a very large house. It is also the best-lit room, because here the public's right was not merely blocked before it could form. It was exercised, in the open, for 143 years, and then withdrawn.

WHAT A READER CAN DO

This is not a problem an individual solves by shopping differently; the bulb is not on the shelf to buy. But the question is now a live one, and ordinary citizens have a way to put it in front of the officials who can act. I have built a set of free, simple online tools that generate a formal, well-documented letter to a reader's governor and state attorney general — the officials with the sovereign standing to challenge federal overreach of this kind in court. The letters are drafted, sourced, and ready; a reader fills in a name and sends. The tools are at marti124.github.io/Patent_Letters, and they take a few minutes to use.

The full record behind this article — the scientific citations, the full text of every Supreme Court case named here, the economic analysis, and the open-book examination the eleven AI models took — is public at marti124.github.io/Patent_Exam and marti124.github.io/Patent_EndNotes.

Return, at the end, to the aisle. An older American stands before forty feet of blue-white light, looking for the warm bulb his family used for as long as anyone remembers. The bulb is the descendant of a design whose patents expired before his grandmother was born. Americans made it and bought it freely from the year his great-grandfather was a boy to the year he himself turned middle-aged. He did nothing to forfeit it. The federal government withdrew it, between 2007 and 2024, on behalf of the companies that profit from its absence.

The Constitution still says what it said when his grandmother screwed a warm bulb into the kitchen fixture and thought nothing of it. Two words — "limited Times" — promised that the bulb would one day be everyone's. It was, for 143 years. It can be again. That is not a matter of nostalgia. It is a matter of enforcing the promise the country already made.

SIDEBAR: HOW A CASE WOULD BE BROUGHT

Why no one challenged it for decades. A reader may ask why, if the violation is this plain, no one sued in fifty years. For most of that time the case could not have been won. The old way to test an unjust law was to break it — manufacture the forbidden bulb, be prosecuted, and argue the Constitution from the defendant's chair — and few businesses will bet their existence on that. The civil alternative, asking a court to strike the rule before anyone is prosecuted, became reliable for a plaintiff in this posture only after a line of Supreme Court decisions between 2007 and 2014. The deeper barrier was Chevron deference: from 1984 until 2024, a court reviewing an agency's reading of an ambiguous statute had to defer if the reading was merely reasonable, so a challenge arguing the Department of Energy had stretched its statute would have lost before it began. On June 28, 2024, the Court overruled Chevron in Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo. Only then did both barriers fall. The long silence is not evidence the case is weak — it is evidence that, until about two years ago, the case was effectively unfilable.

A sound constitutional argument is not the same thing as a filed lawsuit. Someone has to have the right to sue, and someone has to carry the case.

The strongest plaintiffs are the states themselves. A governor or a state attorney general possesses sovereign standing to challenge federal overreach that injures the state and its residents, and several have shown appetite for exactly this kind of suit against federal agencies. A state that sues does not need to prove a personal light-bulb injury; it sues as the sovereign representative of the people harmed within its borders. Tribal nations are a fourth and especially strong candidate: as sovereign governments with a direct, government-to-government relationship to the United States, they hold sovereign, proprietary, and parens patriae standing at once, and each of the 345 federally recognized tribes in the contiguous forty-eight states can decide to file on its own — a breadth of independent plaintiffs no other category matches. County sheriffs, as independently elected constitutional officers, offer a second and less conventional avenue worth testing.

Injured individuals supply a second track. The American whose neurologist has forbidden LED flicker, the patient whose phototherapy depends on a spectrum the rule removed — these are concrete, particularized injuries of the kind that establishes individual standing. Where such individuals are organized into an existing membership or medical organization, that organization may sometimes sue on their behalf under the associational-standing doctrine, provided at least one member could sue in his own right and the interest is germane to the group's purpose.

The case would most naturally be carried by a public-interest law firm of the sort that specializes in constitutional property-rights litigation — the Pacific Legal Foundation is one well-known example of the type — rather than by an individual paying hourly counsel. Firms in this category take constitutional cases on principle and on contingency of mission, have argued and won property and takings cases at the Supreme Court, and possess the appellate experience a question of first impression demands. The role of the citizen, and of a persuaded organization, is to raise the question loudly enough, and document it well enough, that a firm with standing plaintiffs sees a case worth bringing — and that a state official sees a suit worth filing.

None of this requires new legislation, a constitutional amendment, or anyone to break a law. It requires one plaintiff with standing, one competent firm, and one federal courtroom willing to read "limited Times" to mean what it says.

References for the article — every Supreme Court case and source, each with a live link. A companion page collects the full health-science bibliography separately.

Supreme Court Cases Cited

In the order they appear in the article. Each links to the full opinion.

Singer Manufacturing Co. v. June Manufacturing Co., 163 U.S. 169 (1896)
On expiration of a patent, the right to make the invention “becomes public property,” including the right to make the machine in the form in which it was constructed during the patent.
Read the full opinion →
Scott Paper Co. v. Marcalus Manufacturing Co., 326 U.S. 249 (1945)
The patent laws “dedicate to public use” the invention of an expired patent; no one may withhold from the public the use of that invention.
Read the full opinion →
Sears, Roebuck & Co. v. Stiffel Co., 376 U.S. 225 (1964)
An article on which the patent has expired “may be made and sold by whoever chooses to do so.” (Decided with its companion, Compco Corp. v. Day-Brite Lighting, Inc., 376 U.S. 234.)
Read the full opinion →
Kimble v. Marvel Entertainment, LLC, 576 U.S. 446 (2015)
On expiration, “the right to make or use the article, free from all restriction, passes to the public” — the Court’s most recent statement of the public-domain rule.
Read the full opinion →
Bonito Boats, Inc. v. Thunder Craft Boats, Inc., 489 U.S. 141 (1989)
Federal patent law creates a positive federal right to copy and use an expired design; a state may not withdraw from the public domain what patent law has released.
Read the full opinion →
McCormick Harvesting Machine Co. v. Aultman, 169 U.S. 606 (1898)
Once a patent has issued, the agency that granted it has no power to revoke, cancel, or annul it — only a court can. An executive agency cannot set aside what the patent grant secured.
Read the full opinion →
Graham v. John Deere Co. of Kansas City, 383 U.S. 1 (1966)
The Patent Clause is a real limit on Congress: the power to grant patents is bounded by the constitutional command, not an open-ended grant of authority.
Read the full opinion →
Florida Prepaid Postsecondary Education Expense Board v. College Savings Bank, 527 U.S. 627 (1999)
The modern confirmation that a patent is a species of property the law protects — placing the property character of the patent right beyond doubt.
Read the full opinion →
Ruckelshaus v. Monsanto Co., 467 U.S. 986 (1984)
An intangible right of defined economic value is property the Takings Clause protects; an agency does not escape the Clause because what it appropriated cannot be held in the hand.
Read the full opinion →
James v. Campbell, 104 U.S. 356 (1882)
The government’s unauthorized use of a patented invention is a taking of private property for which the inventor must be justly compensated — the Court applying the Fifth Amendment’s “private property” to a patent directly.
Read the full opinion →
Tyler v. Hennepin County, Minnesota, 598 U.S. 631 (2023)
A government may not sidestep the Takings Clause by disavowing a traditional property interest it wishes to appropriate.
Read the full opinion →
United States v. Sioux Nation of Indians, 448 U.S. 371 (1980)
The taking of the Black Hills required just compensation; land held collectively by a people, titled to no individual member, is nonetheless property the Fifth Amendment protects.
Read the full opinion →
Shoshone Tribe of Indians v. United States, 299 U.S. 476 (1937)
Justice Cardozo: “Spoliation is not management.” Property held in common by a people is compensable when the government takes it.
Read the full opinion →
Lucas v. South Carolina Coastal Council, 505 U.S. 1003 (1992)
A regulation destroying all economically viable use of a property right is a taking per se, found without case-by-case balancing.
Read the full opinion →
Penn Central Transportation Co. v. City of New York, 438 U.S. 104 (1978)
The balancing test for regulatory takings that fall short of total deprivation, weighing the severity of the interference and the character of the government action.
Read the full opinion →
Arkansas Game & Fish Commission v. United States, 568 U.S. 23 (2012)
The duration and permanence of a government-imposed burden bear on whether it constitutes a taking.
Read the full opinion →
Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. 369 (2024)
Overruled Chevron deference; courts must again exercise independent judgment in deciding whether an agency has acted within its statutory authority.
Read the full opinion →

Health Science & Regulatory Sources

The numbered notes as they appear in the article body.

[1] Jeffery, Glen, et al. Long-wavelength (670 nm) light exposure and retinal mitochondrial function. Scientific Reports (Nature), 2021.
University College London research documenting measurable improvement in retinal function following brief exposure to deep-red light.
View source →
[2] Jeffery, Glen, et al. Follow-up research on long-wavelength light and cellular function. Scientific Reports (Nature), 2025.
Extends the 2021 findings on the systemic and ocular benefits of deep-red light exposure.
View source →
[3] Hamblin, Michael R. “Mechanisms and applications of the anti-inflammatory effects of photobiomodulation.” AIMS Biophysics 4, no. 3 (2017): 337–361.
Peer-reviewed review documenting how red and near-infrared light are absorbed by mitochondria, raising ATP and reducing inflammation.
View source →
[4] Huberman, Andrew. “Red Light to Improve Metabolism and Harmful Effects of LED Light Exposure | Dr. Glen Jeffery.” Huberman Lab podcast, Stanford School of Medicine.
Discusses Dr. Jeffery’s research on the mitochondrial and retinal benefits of long-wavelength light and the harms of blue-shifted LED exposure.
View source →
[5] Fisher, Robert S., et al. “Visually sensitive seizures: An updated review by the Epilepsy Foundation.” Epilepsia 63, no. 4 (2022): 739–768.
Documents that roughly three percent of people with epilepsy have seizures triggered by flickering light, worst in a low-frequency band some LEDs produce.
View source →
[6] Prevalence figures for epilepsy, seasonal affective disorder, and psoriasis.
Drawn from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, the National Institute of Mental Health, and the National Psoriasis Foundation; full citations in the book’s endnotes apparatus.
View source →
[7] DOE Lighting Market Characterization 2020, U.S. Department of Energy (published April 2024).
Documents approximately 819 million incandescent lamps installed in the U.S. residential sector at the 2020 baseline.
View source →
[8] 42 U.S.C. § 6295(o)(1) — the anti-backsliding provision.
Once an appliance-efficiency standard is set, the Department of Energy may not loosen it. Cornell Legal Information Institute.
View source →
[9] Executive Order 14154, “Unleashing American Energy,” January 20, 2025; 90 Fed. Reg. 8353.
With contemporaneous NEMA and lighting-trade-press confirmation (EdisonReport, Jan. 27, 2025) that the order could not override the statutory lock.
View source →
[10] Cumulative-cost estimate developed in the author’s broader analysis (approximately $22 trillion since 1970; on the order of $3,385 per household per year).
Methodology and sourcing in the book’s public endnotes apparatus.
View source →
